1: May God be gracious to us and bless us, and may he cause his face to shine upon us — Psalm 67 deliberately echoes the Aaronic blessing of Numbers 6:24-26 ("The LORD bless you and keep you; the LORD make his face shine on you and be gracious to you; the LORD turn his face toward you and give you peace"). The connection is not incidental — nearly every phrase of Psalms 67:1 draws from the Num 6 blessing. The significance: what was originally a priestly blessing pronounced over Israel is here turned into a prayer that Israel's blessing might have a missionary purpose. The Aaronic blessing was the beginning of a chain of blessing running outward to all nations.
2: So that your way may be known on earth, your saving power among all the nations — the purpose clause that makes this the Psalter's most explicit missiological text. The blessing of Israel in v1 is explicitly instrumentalized: not so that Israel alone may flourish, but so that YHWH's way (darkekha) and his saving power (yeshuatekha) may be known among all nations. This purpose-structure echoes the original Abrahamic promise: "all peoples on earth will be blessed through you" (Genesis 12:3). Israel's particular blessing exists for the universal goal — a principle the NT applies to the church as Abraham's heirs (Galatians 3:8-9, 14).
3: Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples give you praise — the first occurrence of the refrain that divides the psalm's three stanzas (vv 1-3, 4-5, 6-7). The refrain is imperative: "Let the peoples praise" — it is a call to universal worship, not merely a prediction. Ammim (peoples/nations) in the plural is the standard term for non-Israelite nations. The universal scope is total: "all the peoples" (kol ha-ammim). The refrain was likely sung by the assembled congregation as a response between the leader's verses.
4: For you will judge the peoples with fairness, and guide the nations of the earth — the theological ground for universal praise: YHWH is the judge of all nations, not merely Israel's patron deity. "Judge with fairness" (tishpot ammim mishor) — mishor (uprightness, equity, a level surface) is the quality of unbiased judicial process. The same claim appears in Psalms 9:8; Psalms 96:10; Psalms 98:9; and Isaiah 2:4 (YHWH judging between nations, settling disputes). "Guide the nations" (leummim ba-aretz tanhem) — YHWH as the shepherd-guide of all peoples, not merely Israel. This is the basis on which all nations should rejoice and praise.
5: Let the peoples praise you, O God; let all the peoples give you praise — the refrain appears for the second time at the structural center of the psalm. In the MT, Ps 67 has seven verses; the refrain at vv 3 and 5 creates a chiastic structure with v4 at the center (the universality of YHWH's just governance) and vv 6-7 as the final movement. The Qumran community (11QPsa) preserved this psalm alongside Ps 68, suggesting it was used as a liturgical connector. The doubled refrain marks the psalm's central affirmation.
6: The earth has given its harvest; God, our own God, has blessed us — the harvest as evidence of divine blessing. The statement "God, our own God" (elohim elohenu) is striking: the God who judges all nations (v4) and before whom all peoples should praise (vv 3, 5) is also "our own God" — the covenant God who has a particular relationship with Israel. This covenant particularity is not a contradiction of the universal vision; it is the mechanism of it. YHWH blesses Israel as the concrete, historical demonstration of his universal lordship, so that all ends of the earth may see and fear (v7).
7: God blesses us, and all the ends of the earth will stand in awe of him — the closing verse completes the chain: the Aaronic blessing (v1) → the missionary purpose (v2) → the call to universal praise (vv 3, 5) → the harvest evidence (v6) → the universal fear/awe that results (v7). "All the ends of the earth will stand in awe" (yeiru oto kol afsei aretz) — yare (fear/awe/reverence) in this context is not terror but the reverent recognition of divine sovereignty that prompts worship. The psalm's movement from "grace us and bless us" (v1) to "all the ends of the earth will fear him" (v7) is the complete arc of the Abrahamic covenant's missiological vision.
